title: Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM
learningObjectives: Differentiate JDK, JRE, and JVM clearly
Run javac and java commands from terminal

sections.title: Why Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM matters
sections.content:
Day 1 matters because every Java interview starts with fundamentals. If JDK, JRE, and JVM are not clear, even simple questions become difficult.

In practical work, setup confusion is one of the biggest beginner blockers. A project fails to build, javac is missing, or the wrong Java version runs. Once you know each layer's role, these issues become easy to debug.

Think of it as a workshop:
- JDK = complete toolbox
- JRE = runtime environment
- JVM = execution engine

This clarity helps in both coding and communication. Interviewers evaluate whether you understand what happens after writing code. A strong answer includes compile and run flow, not only definitions.

By the end of this day, you should be able to set up Java on a new machine, run HelloWorld from terminal, and explain runtime flow in simple words.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM
sections.content:
Java is a language plus runtime platform.

### Core terms
- **JDK**: development kit with compiler ('javac') and tools.
- **JRE**: runtime components required to execute Java programs.
- **JVM**: virtual machine that executes bytecode.

### Execution flow
1. Write 'Hello.java'.
2. Compile with 'javac Hello.java'.
3. Compiler outputs 'Hello.class' (bytecode).
4. Run with 'java Hello'.
5. JVM loads and executes bytecode.

### Why this design?
Bytecode allows portability. Same class file can run on different systems with compatible JVM.

### main() signature
'public static void main(String[] args)'
- public: accessible to JVM
- static: callable without object
- void: no return
- args: command-line input

### Setup troubleshooting
- 'java -version' should work
- 'javac -version' should work
- If javac fails: likely JDK not installed or PATH not set.

### Interview-ready statement
"JDK is for development, JRE is for execution, and JVM executes bytecode. Java source compiles to bytecode and runs through JVM."

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**From zero to first run.** Walk through once on your machine: create a folder, add `Hello.java`, run `javac` then `java`, and confirm the `.class` file appears. That muscle memory beats memorizing definitions.

**What the JVM actually does first:** it finds your class, runs static initializers, then calls `main`. Classpath tells it *where* to look; wrong directory → `ClassNotFoundException` even with perfect code.

**JAVA_HOME vs PATH:** PATH is for shells to find `java`/`javac`; JAVA_HOME is the JDK root for Maven, Gradle, and CI. Point both at the *same* JDK version to avoid “compiled with 21, ran with 17” surprises.

**Practice drill:** print `args` from the command line (`java MyApp a b c`) and explain why `args[0]` is `"a"` — interviewers use this to check you understand launch semantics, not only syntax.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.output:
Java setup check
main args count: 0
sections.description: Minimal entry point: prints a message and echoes command-line arguments so you see how args flow into main.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.output:
JDK: compiler (javac), tools, includes JRE
JRE: JVM + libraries to run bytecode
JVM: loads and executes .class bytecode
sections.description: JDK vs JRE vs JVM: use switch expressions to print what each layer is responsible for.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.output: (Exact lines depend on your machine; keys include java.version, java.home, etc.)
sections.description: Runtime diagnostics: read java.version, java.home, and OS so you can debug “wrong Java on PATH” issues.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 1
sections.description: Visual model for Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM
sections.plantuml:
@startuml
title Day 1 - Java Runtime Flow
actor Learner
participant "Java Source (.java)" as SRC
participant "Compiler javac" as C
participant "Bytecode (.class)" as B
participant "JVM" as J
Learner -> SRC : Write code
SRC -> C : Compile
C -> B : Generate bytecode
B -> J : Run
J --> Learner : Output
@enduml

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Installing only JRE and expecting javac to work.
Not restarting terminal after PATH update.
Using wrong class name while running java command.
Confusing source file with compiled class file.
Memorizing terms without understanding execution flow.

sections.title: Exercise - Implement Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM
sections.problem:
Implement a Java program for **Introduction to Java - JDK vs JRE vs JVM** with real input and edge-case handling.

Requirements:
1. Accept at least 5 sample cases.
2. Print clear intermediate output.
3. Include one invalid/boundary case.
4. Explain time complexity in comments.
5. Keep naming and structure interview-ready.
hints: Start with a straightforward solution first.
Write at least one edge case test.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is the JDK and what tools does it include?
conceptual.answer: The JDK (Java Development Kit) is the full development bundle: it includes javac (compiler), jar, javadoc, jdb, and the JRE so you can compile and run Java programs.

conceptual.question: What is the JRE and can you compile Java with only the JRE?
conceptual.answer: The JRE (Java Runtime Environment) contains the JVM and libraries needed to run Java applications. You cannot compile new .java files with JRE alone—you need the JDK for javac.

conceptual.question: What is the JVM’s role?
conceptual.answer: The JVM loads .class bytecode, verifies it, and executes it on the host OS. It provides memory management (heap, GC) and the execution engine for your program.

conceptual.question: What is the relationship between .java, .class, and running a program?
conceptual.answer: You write .java source, javac produces .class bytecode, and the java launcher starts the JVM to execute the bytecode for the named class.

conceptual.question: Why is main() declared public static void main(String[] args)?
conceptual.answer: public so the JVM can call it from outside the class; static so it runs without constructing an object first; void because the JVM does not use a return value; String[] args holds command-line arguments.

conceptual.question: What does “platform independence” mean for Java?
conceptual.answer: Source compiles to bytecode that any compatible JVM can run, so the same .class can run on Windows, Linux, or macOS without recompiling for each CPU instruction set.

conceptual.answer: JAVA_HOME points to the JDK installation root. Maven, Gradle, and IDEs use it to find the compiler and standard libraries consistently across machines.

conceptual.question: What happens if the public class name does not match the file name?
conceptual.answer: For a public top-level class, the file must be named ClassName.java. A mismatch causes a compile-time error.

conceptual.question: What is the difference between compiling with javac and running with java?
conceptual.answer: javac translates source to bytecode files; java starts the JVM and loads the class by name (with package-aware classpath).

conceptual.question: Can two JVM versions behave differently for the same bytecode?
conceptual.answer: Generally bytecode is portable, but behavior can differ if you rely on version-specific APIs, preview features, or different default GC/security settings.

conceptual.question: What is classpath and when does it matter?
conceptual.answer: Classpath tells the JVM where to find .class files and JARs. Wrong classpath causes ClassNotFoundException even if the code compiled.

conceptual.question: What is JIT compilation in the JVM?
conceptual.answer: The JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler translates hot bytecode to native machine code at runtime to speed up frequently executed code after profiling.

conceptual.question: What does bytecode verification do?
conceptual.answer: Before execution, the JVM checks bytecode for type safety, stack consistency, and illegal jumps—reducing certain classes of memory corruption and invalid calls.

conceptual.question: What is the difference between classpath and module path (Java 9+)?
conceptual.answer: Classpath loads classes/JARs the classic way; module path activates the module system (strong encapsulation, reliable configuration). Many apps still use classpath; libraries may publish as modules.

codeBased.answer: Use System.getProperty("java.version") and System.getProperty("java.home") and print them in main.

seniorScenario.question: CI builds with Java 11 but developers use Java 17 locally. What do you standardize?
seniorScenario.answer: Pin the JDK in the build (toolchains, Gradle JVM, Maven enforcer), align CI image and local dev with the same major version, and fail the build on mismatch.

seniorScenario.answer: Verify the main class name, package declaration, classpath, JAR manifest Main-Class entry, and that you run from the correct working directory.

seniorScenario.question: Someone installed JDK but terminal still says javac is not recognized on Windows. What steps?
seniorScenario.answer: Confirm JDK bin path, add it to user/system PATH, set JAVA_HOME to JDK root, open a new terminal, and ensure no conflicting older java earlier in PATH.

seniorScenario.question: You need to run the same JAR on Linux servers with different Java patch levels. What policy?
seniorScenario.answer: Standardize minimum supported LTS, test on lowest supported patch, document required flags, and use container images with pinned JDK versions.

seniorScenario.question: How do you explain JVM vs JDK to a non-technical stakeholder?
seniorScenario.answer: JDK is the full developer kit to build and run; JVM is only the engine that runs the program once built—like needing a factory (JDK) to make a car part, then an engine (JVM) to run the car.

seniorScenario.question: Several JDKs are installed; `java -version` differs from `javac -version`. How do you fix it?
seniorScenario.answer: Ensure PATH lists the same JDK bin for both; on Windows use `where java` and `where javac`; align JAVA_HOME with the intended JDK and remove stale shims.

wrongAnswers: JDK and JVM are the same thing.
You can compile .java files using only the JRE.
Java source code runs directly on the CPU without bytecode.
main can be private static void main and the JVM will still start it.
Classpath is only needed for web applications, not console programs.
The `java` command executes `.java` source files directly without compilation in all Java versions.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Give 1-line meaning first | Keep answer structured | Avoid vague statements |
| Syntax | Show minimal working code | Explain output | Do not skip imports/signature |
| Edge Cases | Always test boundaries | Mention at least one | Most bugs live here |
| Debugging | Print intermediate states | Explain failure reason | Guessing wastes time |
| Quality | Prefer clear names | Talk about readability | Clever but unclear code fails review |
